title: Dynamical preparation of U(1) quantum spin liquids in an analogue quantum simulator
authors: Simon Karch, Melissa Will, Irene Prieto Rodriguez, Nikolas Liebster, SeungJung Huh, Michael Knap, Frank Pollmann, Clemens Kuhlenkamp, Immanuel Bloch, Monika Aidelsburger
abstract: Locally constrained gauge theories underpin our understanding of fundamental interactions in particle physics and the emergent behaviour of quantum materials. In strongly correlated systems, they can give rise to quantum spin liquids that lack conventional order and are defined by coherent superpositions of an extensive number of many-body configurations. Realising and probing such exotic states experimentally is an outstanding challenge both in solid-state and synthetic quantum systems, not least due to the difficulty of detecting the fragile coherences between many-body states. Here, we report a large-scale (>3,000 sites) realisation of a two-dimensional U(1) lattice gauge theory with ultracold atoms in a square optical superlattice and demonstrate non-equilibrium preparation of extended regions of U(1) quantum spin liquids. We demonstrate Gauss's law validity in a quench experiment, enabled by a new microscopy technique for detecting doubly occupied sites. We observe characteristic real-space correlations and momentum-space pinch points, hallmarks of the emergent U(1) gauge structure. Using round-trip interferometric protocols, we directly observe large-scale coherence between many-body configurations, providing strong evidence for quantum spin liquid regions extending over ~100 lattice sites. Our results establish non-equilibrium quantum simulation protocols as a powerful route for accessing and probing exotic, highly-entangled states beyond those hosted by the engineered Hamiltonian in thermal equilibrium.
summary: This paper reports the experimental realization and verification of U(1) quantum spin liquids in a large-scale quantum gas microscope. By using semi-adiabatic ramps, the authors prepare extended regions of the spin liquid and confirm the validity of Gauss's law. They observe hallmark signatures of the U(1) gauge structure, such as pinch points in the structure factor, and use interferometry to prove large-scale many-body coherence. This work establishes non-equilibrium protocols as a vital tool for exploring exotic phases of matter.
main_problem: The challenge of experimentally preparing and verifying the many-body coherence and emergent gauge structure of U(1) quantum spin liquids, which are otherwise difficult to detect due to their fragile nature.
main_result: The researchers successfully prepared extended regions of U(1) quantum spin liquids using non-equilibrium semi-adiabatic ramps and directly observed large-scale many-body coherence and characteristic pinch-point signatures.
method: An analogue quantum simulation using a caesium quantum gas microscope with a 2D optical superlattice, employing semi-adiabatic sweeps and round-trip interferometric protocols for coherence detection.
model_or_system: A large-scale (>3,000 sites) 2D Bose-Hubbard model in a square optical superlattice, which implements an effective 2D monomer-dimer model equivalent to a (2+1)D U(1) lattice gauge theory.
key_observables: Gauss's law validity, real-space dimer-dimer correlations, momentum-space pinch points, and many-body coherence via subsystem return probabilities.
important_parameters: Tunnel coupling J/h approx 130 Hz, staggered potential delta/J approx 12, on-site interaction U/J approx 24, and effective coupling J_eff.
main_assumptions: The Hamiltonian does not support the target RK wavefunction as its ground state, necessitating a non-equilibrium preparation approach; 2D U(1) QSLs are theoretically unstable to perturbations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the monomer-dimer model and preparation protocol; Figure 2 illustrates the experimental setup and Gauss's law verification; Figure 5 details the round-trip interferometric protocol and phase imprinting; Figure 6 presents monomer-monomer correlations and correlation length estimates.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of QSL preparation, describes the experimental platform and effective gauge theory model, details the non-equilibrium preparation and detection methods, presents results on Gauss's law and gauge signatures, and concludes with a quantification of many-body coherence and spatial scales.
why_interesting: It demonstrates a powerful route for accessing highly-entangled, exotic states via non-equilibrium dynamics that are not accessible in thermal equilibrium, providing a blueprint for simulating gauge theories using ultracold atoms.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Effective 2D monomer-dimer model and preparation of U(1) quantum spin liquids. a, Schematic of the 2D monomer-dimer model, where occupied vertices are monomers (green circles) and doublons on links are dimers (blue ellipses). This effective model arises from the 2D Bose-Hubbard model with tunnel coupling J, on-site interaction U ≫J, and staggered superlattice potential ∆≫J, restricting the dynamics to second-order correlated hopping processes |· · · 101 · · ·⟩↔|· · · 020 · · ·⟩ (inset). An additional linear tilt δ/J ∼2 suppresses competing second-order processes such as |· · · 100 · · ·⟩↔|· · · 001 · · ·⟩. b, Starting from the ordered monomer initial state (left, fluorescence...

Figure 2. Probing constrained gauge dynamics in the monomer-dimer model. a, Schematic of the doublon-detection sequence, which converts doubly occupied sites into singly occupied sites prior to imaging. In the presence of a gravitational tilt, reducing the vertical lattice depth converts doublons into singlons with peak fidelity 98(1)%. The lower panel shows the conversion probability p2→1 and the survival probability p1→1 of singlons, as a function of vertical lattice depth. The error bars are smaller than the markers. b, Quantum gas microscope snapshot of a typical time-evolved state for a 47 × 47-site ROI. The inset illustrates the two Gauss’s-law-allowed vertex patterns together with...

Figure 3. Non-equilibrium preparation of U(1) quan- tum spin liquids. a, Schematic energy spectrum as a func- tion of monomer mass m. For large negative monomer mass m, the ground state is unique, with all vertices occupied by monomers and no dimers (left inset). For large positive m, the lowest-energy states form a nearly degenerate manifold of dimer coverings {|di⟩} (right inset), with small splittings aris- ing from higher-order terms such as plaquette interactions. Blue curves are the states connected to the dimer manifold, and red curves represent excited states. b, Schematic of the dynamical mass sweep from m < 0 to m > 0, starting from the monomer initial state. The polar plots...

Figure 4. Experimental preparation of U(1) QSLs. a, Representative experimental snapshot after the forward ramp, with a 35×35-site ROI indicated. Blue shading marks domains of near-perfect dimer covering consistent with the RK wavefunction; red shading marks regions containing monomers and Gauss’s law defects. The probability of a vertex occupied by a single monomer with no attached dimers is 14.9(1)%, and the probability of a single dimer attached to a vertex is 68.4(2)%, giving a Gauss’s law validity of 83.2(2)%. The dominant violation is due to empty sites [9.5(1)%], followed by monomer-plus-dimer [4.3(1)%] and double-dimer [2.7(1)%] configurations. b, Pattern probabilities for subsystem...

Figure 5. Round-trip probe for many-body coherence. a, Schematic of the round-trip interferometric protocol (top). The coupling Jeff (red) is switched on in 1.8 ms and then, the mass is ramped forward into the QSL state. Phase imprinting is optionally applied and the forward ramp is then reversed. Selected fluorescence images (bottom) show the optimal round-trip, in which large initial monomer state domains emerge (left, outlines in red), and a round-trip with a dephasing pulse applied before the reverse ramp that suppresses the appearance of the patches (right). b, Detection probabilities of the two Gauss’s- law-allowed vertex patterns (single monomer, green; single dimer, blue) as a...

Figure 6. Length scale of the QSL regions. a, Subsys- tem return probability L evaluated from round-trip snapshots [Tforward = Treverse = 6.35(3) ℏ/J] as a function of the subsys- tem area A, for rectangular subsystems such as the one illus- trated in the inset (left).
